questions.stem: Which patient should the nurse assess first?

options.text: A patient with new chest pressure and nausea
A patient asking for a pillow
A patient wanting discharge paperwork
A patient asking for ice water

questions.rationale: New chest pressure with nausea may indicate acute cardiac ischemia and must be assessed immediately. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Comfort requests are not the top priority. - C Discharge teaching can wait. - D Water is not urgent compared with possible cardiac symptoms.

distractorRationales.b: "A patient asking for a pillow" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Chest symptoms with nausea are high-priority until proven otherwise" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: Because - C Discharge teaching can wait suggests circulation or perfusion, "A patient wanting discharge paperwork" would not correct the active problem. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: "A patient asking for ice water" moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. The cue "Chest symptoms with nausea are high-priority until proven otherwise" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching.

questions.takeaway: Chest symptoms with nausea are high-priority until proven otherwise.

questions.stem: Which action best helps reduce transmission when caring for a patient with a contagious infection?

options.text: Perform appropriate hand hygiene before and after care
Reuse gloves if they still look clean
Skip precautions if the patient feels better
Share equipment without cleaning it

questions.rationale: Hand hygiene remains one of the most effective infection prevention measures.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses infection progression; "Reuse gloves if they still look clean" moves care toward routine care before infection or sepsis escalation.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of infection or sepsis escalation. The stem cue "Hand hygiene remains one of the most effective infection prevention measures" makes infection progression the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of infection or sepsis escalation. The stem cue "Hand hygiene remains one of the most effective infection prevention measures" makes infection progression the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Simple infection-control steps still matter most.

questions.stem: Which patient should the nurse see first?

options.text: A patient with new chest pressure and diaphoresis
A patient needing help setting up a meal tray
A patient asking when rounds will begin
A patient who wants to know if family can visit later

questions.rationale: Chest pressure with diaphoresis can signal an acute cardiac emergency and must be assessed immediately. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Meal setup is not urgent. - C Rounds timing is nonurgent. - D Visiting questions can wait.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "A patient needing help setting up a meal tray" moves care toward routine care before circulation and perfusion support.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "Chest pressure with diaphoresis can signal an acute cardiac emergency and must be assessed immediately" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: "A patient who wants to know if family can visit later" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "Chest pressure with diaphoresis can signal an acute cardiac emergency and must be assessed immediately" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine observation.

questions.takeaway: Acute chest symptoms outrank routine requests.

questions.stem: Which task is best for the nurse to delegate to AP?

options.text: Transporting a stable patient to radiology
Teaching a patient how to use an inhaler
Evaluating a new complaint of dizziness
Deciding whether chest pain needs an ECG

questions.rationale: Transporting a stable patient is a routine, predictable task that can be delegated when the nurse remains available for assessment follow-up. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Teaching requires nursing knowledge. - C Dizziness requires assessment first. - D Chest pain decisions require nursing judgment and escalation.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it uses teaching before the physiologic risk is controlled. "Transporting a stable patient to radiology" fits the safer response to - D Chest pain decisions require nursing judgment and escalation.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "Evaluating a new complaint of dizziness" moves care toward routine care before circulation and perfusion support.
distractorRationales.d: Because - C Dizziness requires assessment first suggests circulation or perfusion, "Deciding whether chest pain needs an ECG" would not correct the active problem. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.

questions.takeaway: Delegate routine tasks for stable patients, not assessment or teaching.

questions.stem: Which patient should the nurse assess first?

options.text: A patient with chest pain and diaphoresis
A patient asking for a blanket
A patient waiting for discharge teaching tomorrow
A patient requesting help finding a charger

questions.rationale: Chest pain with diaphoresis suggests a potentially unstable cardiovascular event and takes priority over routine comfort or nonurgent requests.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "A patient asking for a blanket" moves care toward routine care before circulation and perfusion support.
distractorRationales.c: "A patient waiting for discharge teaching tomorrow" misses the bedside risk because chest pain with diaphoresis suggests a potentially unstable cardiovascular event and takes priority over routine comfort or nonurgent points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "A patient with chest pain and diaphoresis" fits the safer response to unstable symptoms always come before comfort tasks.

questions.takeaway: Unstable symptoms always come before comfort tasks.

questions.stem: A nurse is monitoring a patient on telemetry when the rhythm suddenly changes to a wide-complex, regular tachycardia at 180 bpm. The patient is alert, with BP 102/60 mmHg and reports palpitations but no chest pain. Which intervention should the nurse anticipate?

options.text: Immediate unsynchronized defibrillation at 200J
Prepare for synchronized cardioversion at 100J after sedation
Administer IV adenosine 6 mg rapid push to terminate the rhythm
Apply a 12-lead ECG, notify the provider, and prepare antiarrhythmic therapy (amiodarone or procainamide) while monitoring hemodynamics closely

questions.rationale: Wide-complex tachycardia (WCT) in a hemodynamically stable patient (alert, BP 102/60, no chest pain) requires accurate rhythm identification before treatment. WCT is most commonly ventricular tachycardia (VT) but can also be supraventricular tachycardia with aberrant conduction (SVT-A). A 12-lead ECG helps differentiate. If VT is confirmed: amiodarone or procainamide IV is the antiarrhythmic of choice for stable VT. Synchronized cardioversion is reserved for hemodynamically unstable stable VT (BP < 90, ischemia, altered mental status). Unsynchronized defibrillation (defib) is for pulseless VT/VF. Adenosine is appropriate for narrow-complex SVT; it is not first-line for wide-complex tachycardia and can be harmful in VT.

distractorRationales.a: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Apply a 12-lead ECG, notify the provider, and prepare antiarrhythmic therapy (amiodarone or procainamide) while monitoring" fits the safer response to stable wide-complex tachycardia: 12-lead first → provider → antiarrhythmic (amiodarone or procainamide for VT).
distractorRationales.b: "Prepare for synchronized cardioversion at 100J after sedation" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Wide-complex tachycardia (WCT) in a hemodynamically stable patient (alert, BP 102/60, no chest pain) requires accurate rhythm identification" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: "Administer IV adenosine 6 mg rapid push to terminate the rhythm" misses the bedside risk because wide-complex tachycardia (WCT) in a hemodynamically stable patient (alert, BP 102/60, no chest pain) requires accurate rhythm identification points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.

questions.takeaway: Stable wide-complex tachycardia: 12-lead first → provider → antiarrhythmic (amiodarone or procainamide for VT). Unstable VT = synchronized cardioversion. Pulseless VT = unsynchronized defibrillation. Never defibrillate a patient who has a pulse.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with acute decompensated heart failure who has a new order for furosemide 40 mg IV push. Which assessment finding is MOST important to report to the provider before administering this medication?

options.text: The patient's weight is 4 lbs above their dry weight
The patient's serum potassium is 3.0 mEq/L
The patient has bilateral 2+ pitting edema of the lower extremities
The patient reports urinary urgency

questions.rationale: Furosemide is a loop diuretic that causes potassium wasting. A pre-dose potassium of 3.0 mEq/L (hypokalemia) is a critical safety concern — administering furosemide will further lower potassium, increasing the risk of life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias (torsades de pointes, ventricular fibrillation), especially in heart failure patients who may be on digoxin. Potassium replacement should be confirmed or ordered before or concurrent with diuresis. Weight gain above dry weight and bilateral edema are the indications FOR furosemide, not contraindications. Urinary urgency is an expected diuretic side effect.

distractorRationales.a: "The patient's weight is 4 lbs above their dry weight" misses the bedside risk because a pre-dose potassium of 3 points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "The patient's serum potassium is 3.0 mEq/L" fits the safer response to this is especially critical in patients on digoxin or with baseline dysrhythmias.
distractorRationales.d: "The patient reports urinary urgency" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "A pre-dose potassium of 3" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine care.

questions.takeaway: Before furosemide: check K+. If < 3.5 mEq/L, notify provider and hold or replace K+ first. Diuretics + hypokalemia = arrhythmia risk. This is especially critical in patients on digoxin or with baseline dysrhythmias.

questions.stem: A patient presents with BP 218/130 mmHg, severe headache, blurred vision, and confusion. Fundoscopic exam shows papilledema. Urinalysis shows proteinuria and hematuria. Which condition is present and what is the correct blood pressure management approach?

options.text: Hypertensive urgency — oral labetalol and discharge home with follow-up
Hypertensive emergency (malignant hypertension) — lower MAP by no more than 25% in the first hour using IV antihypertensives, with careful monitoring for ischemia
Hypertensive emergency — lower BP rapidly to < 120/80 mmHg within 1 hour to prevent further end-organ damage
Severe hypertension without emergency — reassess BP after 30 minutes of rest before intervening

questions.rationale: This patient has hypertensive emergency (malignant hypertension) — severely elevated BP with end-organ damage: neurological (headache, confusion, papilledema = hypertensive encephalopathy), renal (proteinuria, hematuria). This requires IV antihypertensives (labetalol, nicardipine, or clevidipine) with arterial line monitoring. The critical teaching point: do NOT lower BP too rapidly. The target is to reduce MAP by no more than 25% in the first hour. Organs autoregulate to high-pressure environments — a rapid drop can cause ischemic stroke, MI, or renal infarction as perfusion pressure suddenly falls. Hypertensive urgency has severe elevation but NO end-organ damage — treated with oral agents over hours to days.

distractorRationales.a: The option can leave renal injury untreated because it moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. "Hypertensive emergency (malignant hypertension) — lower MAP by no more than 25% in the first hour" fits the safer response to the critical teaching point: do NOT lower BP too rapidly.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses renal injury; "Hypertensive emergency — lower BP rapidly to < 120/80 mmHg within 1 hour to prevent further end-organ damage" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before renal protection.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of renal protection. The stem cue "The critical teaching point: do NOT lower BP too rapidly" makes renal injury the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Hypertensive emergency: severe HTN + end-organ damage = IV antihypertensives, reduce MAP ≤ 25% in hour 1. Then 160/100 over 2-6h. Then normalize over 24-48h. Rapid BP lowering causes ischemia — the brain has autoregulated UP.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with C. difficile (CDI) colitis. Which instruction is MOST important to give the nursing assistant assigned to this patient's room?

options.text: Use alcohol-based hand sanitizer after all patient contact to prevent C. diff spread
Wear gloves and gown for all contact with the patient and environment, and wash hands with soap and water before leaving the room
Wear only gloves since C. diff is transmitted via droplets
Apply an N95 respirator before entering the room due to airborne risk

questions.rationale: C. difficile is transmitted via the fecal-oral route through hardy spores. Key isolation requirements: contact precautions (gloves AND gown for all room entry), and crucially, soap and water handwashing rather than alcohol-based hand rub (ABHR). C. diff spores are NOT killed by alcohol-based sanitizers — soap and water physically removes spores from hands. ABHR is not effective against C. diff. C. diff is not airborne or droplet-transmitted, so N95s are not required. Gloves alone (without gown) are insufficient for contact precautions.

distractorRationales.a: The option can leave infection progression untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Wear gloves and gown for all contact with the patient and environment, and wash hands" fits the safer response to diff is not airborne or droplet-transmitted, so N95s are not required.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses infection progression; "Wear only gloves since C. diff is transmitted via droplets" moves care toward routine care before infection or sepsis escalation.
distractorRationales.d: Because key isolation requirements: contact precautions (gloves AND gown for all room entry) suggests infection progression, "Apply an N95 respirator before entering the room due to airborne risk" would not correct the active problem. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.

questions.takeaway: C. diff = contact precautions (gloves + gown) + soap and water handwashing. Alcohol hand sanitizer does NOT kill C. diff spores. This is one of the few situations where soap/water is explicitly superior to ABHR.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A registered nurse receives a new patient assignment at shift start. The RN reviews the five patients and determines that patient care tasks need to be distributed. Which of the following tasks can be appropriately delegated to a licensed practical nurse (LPN)?

options.text: Perform the admission assessment for a new patient with unstable angina
Develop the care plan for a patient with newly diagnosed type 1 diabetes
Administer a scheduled dose of metoprolol 25 mg PO to a stable cardiac patient with a heart rate of 72 bpm
Educate the patient and family about a new diagnosis of atrial fibrillation

questions.rationale: LPNs/LVNs can administer medications (including PO medications) to stable patients within their scope of practice, following the five rights of medication administration. This is a routine, scheduled medication for a stable patient with an appropriate heart rate — appropriate for LPN delegation. Admission assessment requires comprehensive RN-level assessment skills. Care plan development requires RN clinical judgment and nursing diagnosis formulation. Patient/family education for a new diagnosis requires RN-level assessment of learning needs, teaching, and evaluation — this is an RN function.

distractorRationales.a: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "Administer a scheduled dose of metoprolol 25 mg PO to a stable cardiac patient with a" fits the safer response to patient/family education for a new diagnosis requires RN-level assessment of learning needs, teaching.
distractorRationales.b: Because rN scope: initial assessment, care planning, teaching, evaluating unstable patients suggests circulation or perfusion, "Develop the care plan for a patient with newly diagnosed type 1 diabetes" would not correct the active problem. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: Because rN scope: initial assessment, care planning, teaching, evaluating unstable patients suggests circulation or perfusion, "Educate the patient and family about a new diagnosis of atrial fibrillation" would not correct the active problem. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.

questions.takeaway: LPN scope: administer medications to stable patients, perform routine procedures, collect data for stable conditions. RN scope: initial assessment, care planning, teaching, evaluating unstable patients, and tasks requiring clinical judgment. RNs cannot delegate assessment, teaching, or care planning.
